claims.text: The Truss Links were operator controlled in all physical Truss Link experiments using a custom keyboard interface.
the claim that the ratchet-jointed Truss Link walks 66.5% faster than its plain hinged counterpart ... the ratchet joint's one-way locking mechanism reportedly reverses the ordering entirely
Material cost exceeds $200/unit; the authors say it is neither cheap nor built for mass production

DARPA TRADES: TRAnsformative DESign — a Defense Sciences Office program that ran ~2017-2021 and is now archived; funded computational design tools for exploiting new materials and additive manufacturing, not robotics or military hardware specifically

the VTT relies on a spherical linkage connector that requires careful alignment... the Truss Link uses a free-form magnetic attachment mechanism ... the VTT forms stronger structures than the Truss Link, but lacks the ease of self-assembly of Truss Links (13.7 N pull-away vs. 530 N Spiral Zipper compressive load)
Citations (Semantic Scholar), value: ~5, note: ~1 year post-publication
Citations (Google Scholar), value: ~7, note: ~1 year post-publication
2024-06-24 IEEE ReMAR conference paper; 2024-11-17 arXiv preprint posted; 2025-05-22 accepted at Science Advances; 2025-07-16 published + press embargo lifts

Three tetrahedrons were successfully formed across ten robot-assisted attempts. Getting to the first successful physical tetrahedron self-assembly took 37 manual-tuning attempts before the robot succeeded on its 6th subsequent try
the field still hasn't delivered on two of its three founding promises — robustness and low cost — and that working systems remain capped at roughly 50 modules in practice
physical (motor-driven) experiments in the source paper operated topologies with up to four simultaneous connections per junction, and manually assembled (non-actuated) topologies reached up to six
the same account has separately referenced having written roughly 30 posts on a recurring topic over the preceding year
